title: Tortilla Española
subtitle: Spanish potato omelette
excerpt: Tortilla española is a thick Spanish omelette of potatoes slowly cooked in olive oil and set with eggs. It is served in wedges at room temperature, as a tapa, a light meal, or a picnic staple. The potatoes cook in the oil until very soft before the eggs go in.
sourceNotes: Tortilla española, also called tortilla de patatas, is one of the most widely eaten dishes in Spain. The basic recipe of eggs, potatoes, and olive oil is found across every region. The debate over whether to include onion is ongoing; this version includes it, which is the sweeter, more common form.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made a few hours ahead and served at room temperature. It tastes better once it has cooled completely.

content.text: Tortilla española is a thick set omelette of potatoes and eggs, golden on the outside and custardy within. The potatoes cook very slowly in olive oil until completely soft before the eggs are added. The flip is the only moment of drama; the rest of the method is gentle and patient.
Use a 26 cm non-stick frying pan. The tortilla is served warm or at room temperature, in wedges, and tastes better once it has had a chance to cool and set completely.

items.prepNote: peeled, halved, and sliced 3 mm thick

content.text: Heat {{olive-oil}} in a 26 cm non-stick frying pan over medium-low heat. Add the sliced potatoes and onions. Season with salt. The oil should almost cover the potatoes; if it doesn't, add a little more.

Cook gently for 25 to 30 minutes, turning the potatoes carefully every 5 minutes, until they are very soft, yielding, and translucent but not coloured. They should be just starting to break apart at the edges. Drain into a colander set over a bowl, reserving the olive oil.
Beat the eggs in a large bowl with a pinch of salt. Add the warm potato and onion mixture and stir gently to combine. Leave to rest for 5 minutes so the potatoes absorb some of the egg.

Return 2 tablespoons of the reserved olive oil to the pan over medium heat. Pour in the potato and egg mixture. Cook for 4 to 5 minutes, shaking the pan gently, until the edges are set and the top is still slightly wet.

Place a large flat plate or lid over the pan. Flip the tortilla onto the plate in one confident move, then slide it back into the pan, uncooked side down. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes more until the bottom is golden. The centre should feel slightly soft when pressed; it firms as it cools.
Slide onto a plate and leave to cool for at least 15 minutes before cutting into wedges.

items.symptom: The tortilla broke during the flip
items.cause: Bottom was not set firmly enough before flipping
items.fix: Give it an extra minute with the heat slightly reduced before attempting the flip. The bottom must be firmly set and pulling away from the sides of the pan.

items.symptom: The tortilla is dry and spongy
items.cause: Overcooked, or not enough eggs for the potato volume
items.fix: The centre should still feel slightly soft when you take it off the heat. It firms as it cools. Use 6 eggs for 800 g of potato; fewer eggs and the set is too firm.

items.symptom: The potatoes are crisp rather than soft and yielding
items.cause: Heat too high during the oil-cooking stage
items.fix: The potatoes must cook over medium-low heat, not medium-high. They should soften completely before any browning occurs.

content.text: Tortilla española is eaten at every hour of the Spanish day. It appears as a tapa with a glass of wine at eleven in the morning, as part of a lunch spread, as a sandwich filling, and cold from the fridge the following day. It is one of the dishes most strongly associated with Spanish home cooking and one of the most frequently eaten dishes in Spain.
